## **Coresets for Clustering with Missing Values**

## **Vladimir Braverman**

Johns Hopkins University `vova@cs.jhu.edu`

**Shaofeng H.-C. Jiang** Peking University `shaofeng.jiang@pku.edu.cn`

**Robert Krauthgamer** Weizmann Institute of Science `robert.krauthgamer@weizmann.ac.il`

**Xuan Wu** Johns Hopkins University `wu3412790@gmail.com`

## **Abstract**

We provide the first coreset for clustering points in R _[d]_ that have multiple missing values (coordinates). Previous coreset constructions only allow one missing coordinate. The challenge in this setting is that objective functions, like _k_ -MEANS, are evaluated only on the set of available (non-missing) coordinates, which varies across points. Recall that an _ϵ_ -coreset of a large dataset is a small proxy, usually a reweighted subset of points, that (1 + _ϵ_ )-approximates the clustering objective for every possible center set.

Our coresets for _k_ -MEANS and _k_ -MEDIAN clustering have size ( _jk_ ) _[O]_[(min(] _[j,k]_[))] ( _ϵ[−]_[1] _d_ log _n_ )[2] , where _n_ is the number of data points, _d_ is the dimension and _j_ is the maximum number of missing coordinates for each data point. We further design an algorithm to construct these coresets in near-linear time, and consequently improve a recent quadratic-time PTAS for _k_ -MEANS with missing values [Eiben et al., SODA 2021] to near-linear time.

We validate our coreset construction, which is based on importance sampling and is easy to implement, on various real data sets. Our coreset exhibits a flexible tradeoff between coreset size and accuracy, and generally outperforms the uniformsampling baseline. Furthermore, it significantly speeds up a Lloyd’s-style heuristic for _k_ -MEANS with missing values.

## **1 Introduction**

We consider coresets and approximation algorithms for _k_ -clustering problems, particularly _k_ -MEANS[1] and more generally ( _k, z_ )-CLUSTERING (see Definition 2.1), for points in R _[d]_ with _missing values (coordinates)_ . The presence of missing values in data sets is a common phenomenon, and dealing with it is a fundamental challenge in data science. While data imputation is a very popular method for handling missing values, it often requires prior knowledge which might not be available, or statistical assumptions on the missing values that might be difficult to verify [All01, LR19]. In contrast, our worst-case approach does not requires any prior knowledge. Specifically, in our context of clustering, the distance dist( _x, c_ ) between a clustering center point _c_ and a data point _x_ is evaluated only on the available (i.e., non-missing) coordinates. Similar models that aim to minimize clustering costs using only the available coordinates have been proposed in previous work [HB01, Wag04, CCB16, WLH[+] 19], and some other relevant works were discussed in a survey [HC10].
Clustering under this distance function, which is evaluated only on the available coordinates, is a formidable computational challenge, because distances do not satisfy the triangle inequality, and

> 1In the usual _k_ -MEANS problem (without missing coordinates), the input is a data set _X ⊂_ R _d_ and the goal is to find a center set _C ⊂_ R _[d] , |C|_ = _k_ that minimizes the sum of squared distances from every _x ∈ X_ to _C_ .

35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021).

therefore many classical and effective clustering algorithms, such as _k_ -MEANS++ [AV07], cannot be readily applied or even be defined properly. Despite the algorithmic interest in clustering with missing values, the problem is still not well understood and only a few results are known. In a pioneering work, Gao, Langberg and Schulman [GLS08] initiated the algorithmic study of the _k_ -CENTER problem with missing values. They took a geometric perspective and interpreted the _k_ -CENTER with missing values problem as an affine-subspace clustering problem, and followup work [GLS10, LS13] has subsequently improved and generalized their algorithm. Only very recently, approximation algorithms for objectives other than _k_ -CENTER, particularly _k_ -MEANS, were obtained for the limited case of at most one missing coordinate in each input point [MF19] or for constant number of missing coordinates [EFG[+] 21].

We focus on designing coresets for clustering with missing values. Roughly speaking, an _ϵ_ -coreset is a small proxy of the data set, such that the clustering objective is preserved within (1 _± ϵ_ ) factor for all center sets (see Definition 2.2 for formal definition). Efficient constructions of small _ϵ_ -coresets usually lead to efficient approximations schemes, since the input size is reduced to that of the coreset, see e.g. [HJLW18, FRS19, MF19]. Moreover, apart from speeding up approximation algorithms in the classical setting (offline computation), coresets can also be applied to design streaming [HM04, FS05, BFL[+] 17], distributed [BEL13, RPS15, BLK18], and dynamic algorithms [Cha09, HK20], which are effective methods/models for dealing with big data, and recently coresets were used even in neural networks [MOB[+] 20].

**Experiments.** We implement our algorithm and validate its performance on various real and synthetic data sets in Section 4. Our coreset exhibits flexible tradeoffs between coreset size and accuracy, and generally outperforms a uniform-sampling baseline and a baseline that is based on imputation, in both error rate and stability, especially when the coreset size is relatively small. In particular, on each data set, a coreset of moderate size 2000 (which is 0 _._ 5%-5% of the data sets) achieves low empirical error (5%-20%). We further demonstrate an application and use our coresets to accelerate a Lloyd’s-style heuristic adapted to the missing-values setting. The experiments suggest that running the heuristic on top of our coresets gives equally good solutions (error _<_ 1% relative to running on the original data set) but is much faster (speedup _>_ 5x).

## **1.2 Technical Overview**

Our coreset construction is based on the importance sampling framework introduced by Feldman and Langberg [FL11] and subsequently improved and generalized by [FSS20, BJKW21]. In the framework, one first computes an importance score _σx_ for every data point _x ∈ X_ , and then draws independent samples with probabilities proportional to these scores. When no values are missing, the importance scores can be computed easily, even for general metric spaces [VX12b, FSS20, BJKW21]. However, a significant challenge with missing values is that distances do not satisfy the triangle inequality, hence importance scores cannot be easily computed.

We overcome this hurdle using a method introduced by Varadarajan and Xiao [VX12a] for projective clustering (where the triangle inequality similarly does not hold). They reduce the importancescore computation to the construction of a coreset for _k_ -CENTER objective; this method is quite different from earlier approaches, e.g. [FL11, VX12b, FSS20, BJKW21], and yields a coreset for _k_ -MEANS whose size depends linearly on log _n_ and of course on the size of the _k_ -CENTER coreset. (Mathematically, this arises from the sum of all importance scores.) We make use of this reduction, and thus focus on constructing (efficiently) a small coreset for _k_ -CENTER with missing values.

An immediate difficulty is how to deal with the missing values. We show that it is possible to find a collection of subsets of coordinates _I_ (so each _I ∈I_ is a subset of [ _d_ ]), such that if we construct _k_ -CENTER coresets _SI_ on the data set “restricted” to each _I ∈I_ , then the union of these _SI_ ’s is a _k_ -CENTER coreset for the original data set with missing values. Crucially, we ensure that each “restricted” data set does not contain any missing value, so that it is possible to use a classical coreset construction for _k_ -CENTER. Finally, we show in a technical lemma how to find a collection as necessary of size _|I| ≤_ ( _jk_ ) _[O]_[(min] _[{][j,k][}]_[)] .

Since a “restricted” data set does not contain any missing values, we can use a classical _k_ -CENTER coreset construction, and a standard construction has size _O_ ( _kϵ[−][d]_ ) [AP02], which is known to be tight. We bypass this _ϵ[−][d]_ limitation by observing that actually _O_[˜] (1)-coreset for _k_ -CENTER suffices, even though the final coreset error is _ϵ_ . We observe that an _O_[˜] (1)-coreset can be constructed using a variant of Gonzalez’s algorithm [Gon85].

To implement Gonzalez’s algorithm, a key step is to find the _furthest_ neighbor of a given subset of at most _O_ ( _k_ ) points, and a naive implementation of this runs in linear time, which overall yields a quadratic-time coreset construction, because the aforementioned reduction of [VX12a] actually requires Θ( _n/k_ ) successive runs of Gonzalez’s algorithm. To resolve this issue, we propose a fully-dynamic implementation of Gonzalez’s algorithm so that a furthest-point query is answered in time poly( _k_ log _n_ ), and the point-set is updated between successive runs instead of constructed from scratch. Our dynamic algorithm is based on a random-projection method that was proposed for furthest-point queries in the streaming setting [Ind03]. Specifically, we project the (restricted) data

## **1.3 Additional Related Work**

Coresets for _k_ -MEANS and _k_ -MEDIAN clustering have been studied extensively for two decades, and we only list a few notable results. The first strong coresets for Euclidean _k_ -MEANS and _k_ - MEDIAN were given in [HM04]. In the last decade, most work on coresets for clustering follows the importance sampling framework initiated in [LS10, FL11]. In Euclidean space, recent work showed that coresets for _k_ -MEANS and _k_ -MEDIAN clustering can have size that is independent of the Euclidean dimension [FSS20, SW18, HV20]. Beyond Euclidean space, coresets of size independent of the data-set size were constructed also for many important metric spaces [HJLW18, BJKW21, CASS21]. A more comprehensive overview can be found in recent surveys [Phi17, Fel20].

Recently, attention was given also to non-traditional settings of coresets for clustering, including coresets for Gaussian mixture models (GMM) [LFKF17, FKW19]; simultaneous coresets for a large family of cost functions that include both _k_ -MEDIAN and _k_ -CENTER [BJKW19]; and coresets for clustering under fairness constraints [HJV19]. Also considered were settings that capture uncertainty, for example when each point is only known to lie in a line (i.e., clustering lines) [MF19], and when each point comes from a finite set (i.e., clustering point sets) [JTMF20].

As mentioned in Section 1, we use importance sampling method which is a well-known technique for constructing coresets [FL11, FSS20]. A key step is to compute for every data point _x ∈ X_ an importance score _σx ≥_ 0 that estimates its maximum relative contribution to any solution. The computation of _{σx}_ is standard in metric spaces, see e.g. [FSS20, BJKW21], but this is not applicable for us because distances with missing values do not satisfy the triangle inequality. Hence, we employ an alternative approach proposed by Varadarajan and Xiao [VX12a, Lemma 3.1], which reduces the computation of _{σx}_ to finding coresets for _k_ -CENTER. _k_ **-CENTER coreset for restricted data set via Gonzalez’s algorithm.** Finally, the _k_ -CENTER coreset for the restricted data set on each _I ∈I_ is constructed using an approximate version of Gonzalez’s algorithm [Gon85], where we first pick an arbitrary data point as the initial coreset, and in every iteration an _O_[˜] (1)-approximate furthest neighbor in the dataset is picked into the coreset, and˜ we do this for _k_ times. We show that the resulting coreset, consisting of _k_ + 1 points, is an _O_ (1)-coreset for _k_ -CENTER. The assumption that the input forms a metric space is crucial, and this is guaranteed since we always run on a restricted data set that satisfies the triangle inequality.

**Dynamic implementation of Gonzalez’s algorithm.** To make this _k_ -CENTER coreset construction dynamic, we adapt the random projection technique to Gonzalez’s algorithm. We project the (restricted) point set onto several random lines and use a one-dimensional data structure to construct _k_ -CENTER coreset for each of these (projected) one-dimensional data set. Note that the key step in Gonzalez’s algorithm is finding a furthest neighbor, and we can show that our projection method yields an _O_ ( _k[√]_ log _n_ )-approximation of the furthest neighbor with high probability.

**Datasets.** We run our experiments on three real datasets and one synthetic dataset. Below, we briefly describe how we process and choose the attributes of the dataset, and the parameters of the datasets after processing are summarized in Table 1.

Three columns regarding area contain missing values, and the price column doesn’t contain any missing value.
2. KDDCup 2009 [KDD09] is a dataset on customer relationship prediction. We pick 31 numerical attributes that have similar magnitudes. Each column contains missing values.
3. Vertical farming [Sam21] is a dataset about cubes which are used for advanced vertical farming. We include all of four numerical attributes of the dataset. Each column contains missing values.

4. Synthetic dataset. We generate a large synthetic dataset to validate our algorithm’s scalability. Data points are randomly generated so that 97% of them are in a square and 3% of them are far away from the square. After that, we delete 25% of attributes at random. We remark that the 3% far away points is to make the dataset less uniform which prevents it from being trivial for clustering.

**Implementation notes.** In our experiments, we follow a standard practice of fixing coreset size in each experiment (cf. [BBH[+] 20, JTMF20]). Recall that when computing the importance score, our algorithm chooses a family _I_ of subsets of coordinates and work on each restricted data set _X|I_ for _I ∈I_ (see Section 3.1). For a fixed size coreset, the family size _|I|_ is a parameter that needs to be optimized. ## **4.1 Accuracy of Coresets**

We evaluate the accuracy versus size tradeoff of our coresets. Since the coreset should preserve the clustering cost for _all_ centers, we evaluate the accuracy by testing the _empirical error_ on a selected set of centers _C_ . Namely, for a data set _X_ , a coreset _D ⊆ X_ and a collection of center sets _C_ , we define the empirical error of _D_ as

We use a randomly selected collection of centers _C_ that consists of 100 randomly generated _k_ -subset _C ⊂_ R _[d]_ . Since both the evaluation method and the algorithm has randomness, we run the experiment for _T_ = 10[3] times with independent random bits and report the average empirical error to make it stable. We choose 20 different coreset sizes from 200 to 9700 in a step size of 500, and report the corresponding average empirical error.

**Results.** We report the size versus accuracy tradeoff of our coreset for all four datasets in Figure 2, and record the standard deviation in Figure 3. We compare these results against the abovementioned uniform sampling and imputation baseline. As can be seen from the figures, the accuracy of our coreset improves when the size increases, and we achieve 5%-20% error using only 2000 coreset points (which is within 0 _._ 5% _−_ 5% of the datasets). This 5%-20% error is likely to be enough for practical use, since practical algorithms for _k_ -MEANS are approximation algorithms anyway. Our coresets generally outperform both the uniform sampling and imputation baselines on almost every coreset sample size, and the advantage is more significant when the coreset size is relatively small. Moreover, our coresets have a much lower variance.

We show that our coreset can significantly accelerate this algorithm. Then we run this modified Lloyd’s heuristic again, but on top of our coreset and the uniform sampling baseline respectively, and we compare both the speedup and the relative error[3] against the reference. The experiments are run on the Russian housing data set where the number of iterations of the modified Lloyd’s is set to _T_ = 5000 and the number of clusters is set to a small value _k_ = 3 so as the heuristic is likely to find a local minimum faster. This also helps to explain why improving the size of uniform sampling may not result in a better solution, since as shown in Figure 2a, uniform sampling has a large empirical error (around 50%), so a good solution for the uniform sample may not be a good solution for the original data set.

## **5 Conclusion**

Our coreset construction builds upon the sensitivity-sampling method (cf. [FL11]). However, a central technical challenge is that the standard method to compute the sensitivity scores breaks, because distances between points with missing values do not satisfy the triangle inequality. We overcome this using another known method, of [VX12a], that requires a coreset for _k_ -CENTER. Our main innovation is a near-linear time algorithm that computes an _O_ (1)-approximate _k_ -CENTER coreset for points with missing values. To this end, we need the following key steps, which constitute our main technical contribution.

- We reduce the _k_ -CENTER coreset construction with missing values, to the construction of traditional _k_ -CENTER coresets (i.e., without missing values) on a series of instances. These instances are built by restricting data points with missing values to a carefully-chosen collection of subspaces. The guarantee needed from this collection is a certain combinatorial structure, and we indeed prove it exists.

- The method of Varadarajan and Xiao executes the _k_ -CENTER coreset algorithm many times, and overall takes quadratic time. To improve the running time, we design an efficient dynamic algorithm for the well-known Gonzales’ algorithm (which computes an _O_ (1)approximate _k_ -CENTER coreset). The main idea in this dynamic algorithm is to project the data points onto (data-oblivious) random 1D lines, and build on each line a dynamic data structure that supports furthest-neighbor queries (in 1D).
Finally, we implemented our algorithm and the experiments indicate that our algorithm is efficient and accurate enough to be potentially applicable in practice.

**Future directions.** As an immediate follow-up, one could try to improve our coreset size, e.g., removing the dependence in log _n_ . Our input can be viewed as axis-parallel affine-subspaces. Hence, another an interesting direction is to obtain coresets for the more general setting where the input consists of general affine-subspaces.

**Potential negative societal impacts.** Our paper focuses on computational issues (improving time and space) of known clustering tasks. Clustering methods in general have potential issues with fairness and privacy, which applies also to our work, but our research is not expected to introduce new negative societal impact beyond what is already known.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper presents a new coreset construction for k-means and k-median clustering with missing values, significantly improving the runtime for approximation schemes in this setting. The authors combine existing importance sampling frameworks with a novel dynamic k-center coreset construction to handle the lack of triangle inequality when coordinates are missing.

The paper provides a strong theoretical contribution by extending coreset techniques to a challenging, non-metric clustering setting. The construction of a dynamic k-center coreset is clever and well-motivated. The empirical results demonstrate clear practical utility, making this a solid contribution to the field.

Overview issues:

- Ambiguity in the definition of the (j,k,d)-family: Definition 3.3 and Lemma 3.4 are central to the paper's ability to handle missing values by restricting data to subspaces. However, the definition of a (j,k,d)-family is slightly opaque regarding the dependence on the number of missing values j. Specifically, it is unclear if the family size O((j+k)^{j+k+1} / (j^j k^k) log d) is tight or if it can be optimized for specific missingness patterns. The authors should clarify if this construction assumes that the missingness pattern is fixed or if it must hold for any subset of size j. Providing a small, concrete example of this family for d=4, j=2, k=2 would greatly assist reader understanding.

- Dependence on the 'constant probability' guarantee: Theorem 1.1 and Lemma 3.2 rely on 'constant probability' success. In many applications of coresets, especially those used in downstream machine learning pipelines, the failure probability needs to be tunable (e.g., 1-delta). The paper does not explicitly discuss how the coreset size scales with 1/delta. It would be beneficial to add a discussion or a corollary showing the dependence of the coreset size m on log(1/delta) to ensure the construction is robust for practical use cases.

- Sensitivity to the 'j-point' assumption: The paper assumes that every data point has at most j missing values. In real-world datasets, missingness is often non-uniform, where some points might have very few missing values while others have many. The current construction treats all points as if they have exactly j missing values for the sake of the combinatorial bounds. The authors should discuss how the coreset construction would adapt if j were replaced by a distribution of missingness across the dataset, or if the bound could be tightened by using the average number of missing values instead of the maximum.

- Lack of theoretical analysis for the Lloyd's-style heuristic: Section 4.2 describes a modified Lloyd's heuristic and provides empirical evidence of its speedup. While the empirical results are compelling, the paper lacks a theoretical explanation for why this heuristic performs well on the coreset. Since the objective function with missing values does not satisfy the triangle inequality, the convergence guarantees of standard Lloyd's do not apply. A brief discussion or a heuristic argument explaining why the coreset preserves the local minima structure of the original dataset would strengthen the paper's claims.

- Demonstration of the coreset property on a non-trivial synthetic DGP: The paper provides empirical validation on real-world datasets, but these datasets have unknown ground-truth clustering structures and missingness mechanisms. To demonstrate that the (1+epsilon)-approximation guarantee holds in a controlled environment, the paper should include a simulation using a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) where the true cluster centers are known. Specifically, generating points from k well-separated Gaussians and then masking coordinates according to an adversarial or non-uniform missingness pattern would allow for a precise calculation of the true objective function. Comparing the coreset-based estimate against the true objective value for a range of center sets would verify the theoretical (1+epsilon) claim more rigorously than the current heuristic-based evaluation.

- Clarification of the coreset size's dependence on the success probability: The main theorem states that the coreset construction succeeds with 'constant probability', but it does not provide the standard amplification mechanism to achieve a failure probability of delta. For practitioners, this is a major omission because the coreset size is often the primary bottleneck for memory and runtime. The authors should derive the dependence of the coreset size m on log(1/delta) and include this in the statement of Theorem 1.1. Providing this scaling factor is essential for the paper to claim that the coreset is a robust tool for large-scale data science applications.

- Theoretical justification for the Lloyd's heuristic performance: The paper reports significant speedups for a Lloyd's-style heuristic on the coreset, but the theoretical link between the coreset property and the convergence of this heuristic is missing. Because the distance function lacks the triangle inequality, it is not immediately obvious that a (1+epsilon)-approximation of the objective function implies that the local minima found by Lloyd's on the coreset are close to those on the original dataset. A brief analysis or a formal argument explaining why the coreset preserves the 'local structure' of the objective function, or why the Lloyd's update step remains stable under the coreset weighting, would bridge the gap between the empirical success and the theoretical guarantees.

Recommendation: minor revision. The paper is theoretically sound and empirically grounded, but it requires clarification on the combinatorial bounds and a more robust treatment of the failure probability to be fully ready for a top-tier venue.

Revision targets:

- Provide a concrete example of the (j,k,d)-family construction for small parameters to clarify Definition 3.3.

- Explicitly derive the dependence of the coreset size m on the failure probability delta.

- Add a brief discussion on how the coreset construction behaves when the number of missing values per point is not uniform, addressing the 'j-point' assumption limitation.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a novel coreset construction for k-Means and k-Median clustering with missing values, addressing a gap in handling multiple missing coordinates. The theoretical framework combines importance sampling with a dynamic k-CENTER coreset construction, yielding near-linear time complexity. While the approach is algorithmically interesting, the manuscript requires stronger justification for the approximation guarantees of the dynamic furthest-point oracle, more rigorous empirical validation of the coreset property, and clearer discussion of the practical implications of its exponential dependence on j and k.

The authors tackle a non-trivial algorithmic challenge by adapting coreset techniques to a setting where the triangle inequality fails. The reduction to restricted coordinate spaces and the dynamic implementation of Gonzalez's algorithm show technical creativity. The work builds a solid foundation for faster clustering with missing data, but the theoretical guarantees and experimental validation need tightening before publication.

Overview issues:

- Approximation degradation in dynamic Gonzalez's algorithm: Lemma 3.5 guarantees an $O(k\sqrt{\log n})$-approximation for furthest-neighbor queries using random projections. Gonzalez's algorithm, however, relies on picking the exact furthest point at each iteration to maintain its constant-factor approximation guarantee for k-CENTER. Substituting exact queries with approximate ones can compound errors across iterations, potentially invalidating the claimed $\tilde{O}(1)$-coreset size. The authors should either prove that the error does not accumulate destructively in this specific dynamic setting, or adjust the coreset size bound to account for the approximation factor of the furthest-point oracle.

- Insufficient evaluation of the coreset approximation guarantee: The empirical error metric in Section 4.1 evaluates the coreset on only 100 randomly generated center sets. A coreset must preserve the clustering cost for every possible center set, not just a random sample. Testing on random centers does not capture worst-case deviations and leaves a gap between the theoretical $(1\pm\epsilon)$ guarantee and the experimental validation. The authors should include an evaluation against a larger, more structured set of centers, such as those derived from k-Means++ or adversarial placements, to demonstrate that the coreset maintains accuracy across diverse cluster configurations.

- Disconnect between theoretical bounds and practical runtime claims: The coreset size and construction time both contain the factor $(jk)^{O(\min(j,k))}$. While this is polynomial when j or k is fixed, it grows exponentially in the other parameter. The paper describes the construction as near-linear time, which is asymptotically correct with respect to n but hides this exponential dependence. In Section 4, experiments on the KDD cup dataset use $j=30$, where the theoretical bound would be astronomically large. The authors should clarify that the near-linear claim strictly refers to the dependence on n and d, and discuss how the algorithm behaves when j or k exceeds the small-constant regime assumed in the theory.

- Lack of robustness analysis across missingness mechanisms: The theoretical construction assumes a worst-case bound on the number of missing coordinates j, but it does not account for the structure of the missingness pattern. Real-world data often exhibits correlated or non-random missingness (MNAR), which can create dense regions of available coordinates that violate the uniform coverage assumptions implicit in the $(j,k,d)$-family construction. The experiments only test random or column-wise missingness. Adding a discussion or simulation on how correlated missingness affects the required family size $|\mathcal{I}|$ and coreset accuracy would strengthen the practical relevance of the method.

- Verification of sensitivity bounds across coordinate restrictions: The importance sampling framework requires computing sensitivity scores that upper-bound the relative contribution of each point to the k-Means objective. The paper reduces this to k-CENTER coresets on restricted coordinate sets $X_{|I}$. However, a point's sensitivity in the full space $\mathbb{R}^d$ depends on distances evaluated over its available coordinates $I_x$. The union of coresets over $I \in \mathcal{I}$ must ensure that for any center C, the sensitivity computed on the restricted spaces correctly bounds the contribution over $I_x$. The authors should explicitly verify that the sensitivity scores derived from the $X_{|I}$ instances do not underestimate the true k-Means sensitivity, as an underestimation would break the $(1\pm\epsilon)$ approximation guarantee.

- Missing comparison to the prior quadratic PTAS: The introduction and abstract explicitly state that the coreset construction improves the quadratic-time PTAS of Eiben et al. (SODA 2021) to near-linear time. The experiments, however, only benchmark against Lloyd's heuristic, uniform sampling, and an imputation baseline. The paper never reports runtimes or accuracy results for the Eiben et al. algorithm, nor does it show the actual speedup achieved by plugging the coreset into that PTAS. To substantiate the claimed algorithmic improvement, the authors should add a table comparing the wall-clock time and objective value of the PTAS with and without the coreset. This direct comparison is necessary to verify that the near-linear construction actually yields a practical speedup over the previous state-of-the-art.

- No empirical validation of the near-linear construction time: The theoretical analysis guarantees a construction time of $	ilde{O}(n)$, but Section 4 provides no timing measurements for the coreset construction algorithm itself. The only runtime data presented concerns the Lloyd's heuristic running on the coreset. Readers cannot verify whether the construction phase actually scales linearly with the dataset size or if hidden constants dominate for the tested parameters. The authors should report construction times across a range of dataset sizes $n$ and plot them against the theoretical bound. Including these measurements would bridge the gap between the asymptotic analysis and the practical implementation.

- Missing worked example for the coordinate family construction: The coreset size bound hinges on the size of the $(j,k,d)$-family of coordinate subsets, which the authors prove exists in Lemma 3.4 but never instantiate. The paper lacks a concrete, fully-specified example showing how the family is constructed and how many subsets it contains for small values of $j, k,$ and $d$. Computing this family for a simple case, such as $d=3, j=1, k=2$, would allow readers to calibrate their intuition about the combinatorial overhead. Providing this worked example would clarify how the theoretical bound translates to actual coreset sizes and help practitioners choose parameters in practice.

Recommendation: Major revision. The core algorithmic idea is promising, but the manuscript contains a potential proof gap regarding the dynamic furthest-point oracle's impact on coreset quality, and the empirical validation does not rigorously test the coreset property. Addressing these theoretical clarifications and expanding the experimental evaluation are necessary to meet top-venue standards.

Revision targets:

- Provide a rigorous proof or counterexample demonstrating that using an $O(k\sqrt{\log n})$-approximate furthest point in Gonzalez's algorithm preserves the $\tilde{O}(1)$-coreset guarantee, or adjust the coreset size bound accordingly.

- Expand the empirical evaluation to test coreset accuracy against a worst-case or algorithm-generated set of centers, rather than relying solely on 100 random centers.

- Clarify the practical runtime implications of the $(jk)^{O(\min(j,k))}$ factor and explicitly state that the near-linear claim applies only to the dependence on n and d.

- Include a discussion or additional experiments analyzing how correlated or adversarial missingness patterns affect the required family size $|\mathcal{I}|$ and overall coreset performance.

Please correct $T = 5000$ to a standard iteration limit or provide a clear justification for why such an excessive count is necessary on this dataset.

comments.title: Unjustified approximation factor in Lemma 3.5
comments.quote: we can show that our projection method yields an $O(k\sqrt{\log n})$-approximation of the furthest neighbor with high probability.
comments.explanation: Standard random projection bounds typically preserve pairwise distances within a factor of $1 	ext{pm} 	ext{epsilon}$, yielding an approximation factor of $O(	ext{sqrt}	ext{log} n)$ for a fixed set of points. The linear $k$ factor in $O(k	ext{sqrt}	ext{log} n)$ is unusually large and likely stems from a loose union bound or a specific limitation of projecting onto $O(k 	ext{log} n)$ lines. Without a derivation or citation, this factor weakens the theoretical guarantee. Please add a brief derivation or citation justifying the $O(k	ext{sqrt}	ext{log} n)$ factor in Lemma 3.5, or tighten the analysis to show the factor can be reduced to $O(	ext{sqrt}	ext{log} n)$ without affecting the construction time.

coarse__glm-4.7-flash.label: Coarse (glm-4.7-flash)
coarse__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback:
This paper presents coresets for $k$-Means and $k$-Median clustering when data points have multiple missing coordinates. The authors improve upon prior quadratic-time PTAS results by constructing these coresets in near-linear time using a reduction to $k$-Center and a dynamic implementation of Gonzalez's algorithm.

The work makes a meaningful contribution to a niche but important problem in data science. The theoretical framework is sound, building on established techniques like importance sampling and Varadarajan & Xiao's reduction. However, the presentation of the core technical mechanism is incomplete, and the experimental validation misses a key claimed application.

Overview issues:

- Theoretical bound on coreset size is not tight with respect to experiments: Theorem 3.1 states the coreset size is $(jk)^{O(\min(j,k))}$, which implies an exponential dependence on the number of missing coordinates $j$. For moderate values like $k=5$ and $j=30$, this bound becomes astronomically large. However, the experiments (e.g., on the KDD cup dataset with $j=30$) show success with practical coreset sizes. This disconnect between the theoretical bound and the experimental results weakens the claim that the construction is 'near-linear' for high $j$. The paper does not explain why the bound is loose or how to handle regimes where it is tight.

- Technical overview fails to explain the core reduction mechanism: Section 1.2 is titled 'Technical Overview' and promises to explain the algorithm's key steps. It describes the *approach* (importance sampling) but skips the *mechanism* for handling missing values. It relies entirely on the reader understanding Lemma 3.2 to grasp how the reduction to $k$-Center works. A high-level description of the reduction step—specifically how importance scores are computed using the $k$-Center coreset—is missing from the overview, making the paper difficult to verify.

- Experiments lack a streaming validation: The introduction claims coresets enable streaming algorithms and mentions the 'merge-and-reduce' method in Section 1.1. However, the experiments only test static data. A streaming simulation (inserting points one by one and maintaining the coreset) would validate the 'near-linear' construction claim and demonstrate the full utility of the dynamic algorithm. Without this, the paper misses an opportunity to showcase the practical benefits of the dynamic implementation described in the proof.

- Imputation baseline lacks specific implementation details: Section 4.1 compares against an 'imputation-based baseline' but does not specify the imputation method used (e.g., random values, mean, median). While the empirical results are good, the paper does not explain *why* imputation fails in this setting (e.g., loss of variance in restricted dimensions). Discussing the theoretical limitations of imputation would strengthen the argument that coresets are a fundamentally better approach for this specific distance metric.

Recommendation: major revision. The core theoretical contribution is valid, but the paper is incomplete as a conference submission. The Technical Overview fails to explain the critical reduction mechanism, and the experiments do not validate the claimed streaming utility.

Revision targets:

- Add a streaming simulation to the experiments to validate the 'near-linear' construction claim.

- Expand the Technical Overview in Section 1.2 to explicitly explain the reduction mechanism for computing importance scores.

- Clarify the imputation baseline details in Section 4.1, specifying the method used and discussing its theoretical limitations.

- Address the discrepancy between the theoretical bound and experimental results by explaining why the bound is loose or how it applies to real-world data.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing regarding coreset size
comments.quote: we achieve  $5\% - 20\%$  error using only 2000 coreset points (which is within  $0.5\% - 5\%$  of the datasets).
comments.explanation: The phrase "within ... of the datasets" is awkward and mathematically imprecise. It likely intends to state that the coreset size is a small fraction of the original dataset size. Rewrite to clarify this comparison, for example: 'using only 2000 coreset points (which is within $0.5\% - 5\%$ of the dataset size).'

comments.title: Discrepancy between theoretical guarantee and empirical test
comments.quote: resets. Since the coreset should preserve the clustering cost for all centers, we evaluate the accuracy by testing the empirical error on a selected set of centers  $\mathcal{C}$ . Namely
comments.explanation: The text claims the coreset preserves the clustering cost for *all* centers, yet the evaluation only tests a random subset. Clarify that this is an empirical check of the theoretical guarantee, not a proof of it.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
This paper presents the first coreset construction for clustering with multiple missing values, a significant theoretical contribution. The technical approach—reducing to k-center coresets via a combinatorial coordinate family and designing a dynamic Gonzalez algorithm—is novel and well-motivated. The experimental validation, while limited in scope, demonstrates practical promise. However, several issues in the theoretical analysis, experimental design, and framing need to be addressed before the paper meets the standards of a top venue.

Overview issues:

- Sensitivity bounds and importance sampling analysis are not fully presented: The paper relies on the importance sampling framework of Feldman and Langberg, but the sensitivity bounds for the missing-values setting are not derived in the main text. Theorem 3.1 states the coreset size but the proof sketch omits the crucial step of bounding the sum of sensitivities (the total importance score). Without this derivation, the reader cannot verify that the coreset size bound is correct. The authors should either include the full sensitivity analysis in the main paper or clearly reference where it appears in the full version, and explain how the missing-values setting affects the standard sensitivity bounds.

- The dynamic k-center coreset construction has unverified assumptions: Lemma 3.2 claims a dynamic algorithm maintaining an O(k sqrt(d log q))-coreset for k-center with missing values. However, the proof sketch relies on Lemma 3.5, which assumes the restricted data sets satisfy the triangle inequality (which they do) but also requires that the random projection technique yields an O(k sqrt(log n))-approximation to the furthest neighbor. The analysis of this approximation factor depends on properties of Gaussian projections that may not hold when distances are computed only over a subset of coordinates (even if the restricted data is fully observed). The authors should verify that Lemma 3.5 extends to the setting where the point set A is a restricted data set X_{|I} with arbitrary geometry, and clarify the dependence on the dimension of the restricted space.

- The coreset size bound may be impractical for realistic j and k: The coreset size is (jk)^{O(min(j,k))} * poly(epsilon^{-1} d log n). For j=10 and k=10, the base factor is (100)^{10} = 10^20, which is astronomically large. The paper acknowledges this exponential dependence in Theorem 1.2 as unavoidable in the worst case, but the experimental section uses only small j and k (j=3, k=3; j=30, k=5; j=4, k=2; j=3, k=3). The paper should include experiments with larger j and k (e.g., j=10, k=10) to demonstrate that the coreset remains practically useful, or explicitly discuss the regimes where the coreset is feasible. Without such discussion, the practical relevance of the theoretical bound is unclear.

- The PTAS speedup claim is not fully substantiated: Corollary 1.3 claims a near-linear time PTAS by combining the coreset with the Eiben et al. PTAS. However, the runtime of the Eiben et al. PTAS is O(2^{poly(jk/epsilon)} * n^2 d). When run on the coreset of size m, the quadratic dependence becomes O(2^{poly(jk/epsilon)} * m^2 d). Since m itself depends exponentially on j and k, the overall runtime may still be dominated by the exponential term, not the near-linear term. The authors should provide a more detailed analysis of the combined runtime, showing that the coreset construction time dominates and that the PTAS on the coreset is indeed faster than running it on the original data. A concrete numerical example comparing runtimes for typical parameters would be helpful.

- Experimental evaluation lacks comparison to the previous PTAS and strong baselines: The experiments compare the coreset only against uniform sampling and a simple imputation baseline. The paper claims to improve the Eiben et al. PTAS to near-linear time, but no comparison to that PTAS is provided—not even on small datasets where the PTAS might be runnable. Additionally, the imputation baseline is weak: filling missing values with random numbers is unlikely to preserve cluster structure. The authors should compare against a more principled imputation method (e.g., k-nearest neighbors imputation or matrix completion) and, if possible, against the Eiben et al. PTAS on small datasets. Without these comparisons, the claimed superiority of the coreset is not convincingly demonstrated.

- The Lloyd's heuristic speedup experiment conflates coreset quality with algorithm convergence: Section 4.2 shows that running Lloyd's on the coreset achieves <1% relative error with 5x speedup. However, the Lloyd's heuristic is known to converge to local minima, and running it on a coreset may change the landscape of local minima. The paper does not discuss whether the coreset preserves the global optimum or whether the heuristic finds a different local minimum. The low relative error could be due to the heuristic finding a solution that is close to the original data's local minimum by chance, rather than due to coreset quality. The authors should either provide a theoretical guarantee that the coreset preserves the set of near-optimal solutions for Lloyd's, or run the heuristic from multiple random initializations to demonstrate that the coreset does not systematically lead to worse local minima.

- Missing concrete worked example for coreset construction: The paper claims to provide the first coreset for clustering with multiple missing values, but it never works through a single fully-specified example. For instance, the authors could take a tiny dataset with n=4 points in d=2 dimensions, each missing at most j=1 coordinate, and explicitly compute the sensitivity scores, the (j,k,d)-family, the restricted k-center coresets, and the final coreset. This would let readers calibrate their intuition for how the construction operates and verify that the claimed size bound (jk)^{O(min(j,k))} is not vacuous. Without such an example, the reader cannot assess whether the construction is implementable as described or whether the bounds are meaningful in practice.

- No simulation demonstrating coreset validity for a known ground-truth DGP: The paper validates its coreset on real datasets where the true clustering structure is unknown, but it never tests on a synthetic dataset where the correct answer is known. A standard evaluation in the coreset literature is to generate data from a mixture of k Gaussians (or a k-means ground truth), introduce missing coordinates according to a known mechanism, and then verify that the coreset preserves the optimal clustering cost within 1+epsilon. The authors should add such a simulation, reporting the approximation ratio achieved for several epsilon values and coreset sizes. Without this, the reader cannot distinguish whether the low empirical error on real data reflects coreset quality or simply the fact that many center sets yield similar costs.

- PTAS speedup claim lacks runtime comparison with original PTAS: Corollary 1.3 claims a near-linear time PTAS by combining the coreset with the Eiben et al. PTAS, but the paper provides no runtime comparison—not even a back-of-the-envelope calculation for a concrete parameter setting. The Eiben et al. PTAS has runtime O(2^{poly(jk/epsilon)} * n^2 d), and the coreset size m is (jk)^{O(min(j,k))} * poly(epsilon^{-1} d log n). For j=5, k=5, epsilon=0.1, the exponential term 2^{poly(25)} is enormous, while m might be manageable. The authors should compute the actual runtime of the combined algorithm for a few representative (j,k,epsilon) triples and compare it to the runtime of running the Eiben et al. PTAS directly on the original data. Without this comparison, the claimed improvement is not substantiated.

- No discussion of practical coreset construction for large j or k: The coreset size bound is (jk)^{O(min(j,k))}, which for j=10, k=10 is (100)^{10} = 10^20 — far beyond any practical implementation. The paper acknowledges this exponential dependence in Theorem 1.2 as unavoidable in the worst case, but it never discusses what parameter regimes make the coreset practically useful. The experiments only use j <= 4 and k <= 5, which are far from the worst case. The authors should add a section discussing the practical limitations of the coreset size, including a table of the coreset size for various (j,k) pairs (e.g., j=2,5,10,20 and k=2,5,10,20) and the corresponding memory and time requirements. Without this discussion, a reader cannot determine whether the coreset is applicable to their problem.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Include the full sensitivity analysis for the importance sampling bounds, either in the main paper or with a clear reference to the full version, and explain how missing values affect the standard bounds.

- Provide a rigorous analysis of the random projection lemma (Lemma 3.5) in the context of restricted data sets, and verify that the approximation factor holds for arbitrary subsets of coordinates.

- Add experiments with larger j and k (e.g., j=10, k=10) to demonstrate practical feasibility, or explicitly discuss the parameter regimes where the coreset is useful.

- Compare the coreset against the Eiben et al. PTAS on small datasets and against a stronger imputation baseline (e.g., k-NN imputation), and provide a detailed runtime analysis of the combined coreset+PTAS approach.

- Discuss the impact of coreset on Lloyd's heuristic convergence, and run the heuristic from multiple random initializations to ensure the speedup does not come at the cost of solution quality.

comments.title: Lemma 3.5 is ill-defined and the inequality is not properly stated
comments.quote:
Lemma 3.5.

Let $A\subset\mathbb{R}^{d}$, $|A|=n$, $\delta>0$ and integer $k\geq 1$. Let $\mathcal{V}$ be a collection of $O(k\log n+\log\frac{1}{\delta})$ random vectors, each drawn independently from $\mathcal{N}(0,I_{d})$. Then with probability at least $1-\delta$, for every $P\subseteq A$ and $Q\subseteq A$, $|Q|\leq k$, if $p^{\star}$ is a furthest point in $P$ from $Q$, and for every $v\in\mathcal{V}$ we let $\langle p^{v},v\rangle$ be a furthest point in $\langle P,v\rangle$ from $\langle Q,v\rangle$, then

$\mathrm{dist}(p^{\star},Q)\leq O(k\sqrt{\log n})\cdot\max_{v\in\mathcal{V}}\mathrm{dist}(p^{v},Q),$
comments.explanation: The notation $\langle p^{v},v\rangle$ suggests $p^v$ is a point in $P$, but the definition says 'for every $v\in\mathcal{V}$ we let $\langle p^{v},v\rangle$ be a furthest point in $\langle P,v\rangle$ from $\langle Q,v\rangle$'. This implies $p^v$ depends on $v$, not on a superscript. More critically, the inequality compares $\mathrm{dist}(p^{\star},Q)$ (a distance in $\mathbb{R}^d$) with $\mathrm{dist}(p^v, Q)$ (also in $\mathbb{R}^d$), but the right-hand side should involve projected distances, i.e., $|\langle p^v, v\rangle - \langle q, v\rangle|$ for some $q\in Q$. The lemma as written is not well-defined. Rewrite it to clearly define $p^v$ as a point in $P$ that maximizes the projected distance, and state the inequality in terms of projected distances. Alternatively, provide a reference to a standard random projection lemma that gives a similar guarantee.

comments.title: Proof of Lemma 3.3 is incomplete — only one side of the coreset definition is shown
comments.quote: Suppose $\mathcal{I}$ is a $(j,k,d)$-family Let $X\subseteq\mathbb{R}^{d}_{?}$ be a set of $j$-points, and for every $I\in\mathcal{I}$, let $Y_{I}$ be an $\alpha$-coreset for $k$-Center on $X_{|I}$. Then $\cup_{I\in\mathcal{I}}Y_{I}^{-1}$ is an $\alpha\sqrt{d}$-coreset for $k$-Center on $X$.
comments.explanation: The proof shows that for any $x\in X$ and center set $C$, if $\mathrm{dist}(x,C) \geq r$, then there exists $y\in \cup_{I\in\mathcal{I}} Y_I^{-1}$ with $\mathrm{dist}(y,C) \geq r/(\alpha\sqrt{d})$. However, the definition of an $\alpha$-coreset requires that for every center set $C$, the maximum distance from points in $X$ to $C$ is approximated within factor $\alpha$ by the maximum distance from points in the coreset to $C$. The proof only provides a one-sided bound (lower bound on the coreset's maximum distance). It does not show that the coreset's maximum distance is at most $\alpha\sqrt{d}$ times the original maximum distance, nor does it handle the case where the original maximum distance is achieved by a point not covered by the argument. Add a symmetric argument to bound the coreset's maximum distance from above, or clarify that the definition of coreset used here is one-sided (which would be non-standard).

comments.title: Corollary 1.3 runtime bound omits the coreset size factor in the PTAS term
comments.quote: There is an algorithm that, given $0<\epsilon<1/2$, integers $d,j,k\geq 1$, and a set $X\subset\mathbb{R}^{d}$ of $n$ points each having at most $j$ missing values, it finds with constant probability a $(1+\epsilon)$-approximation for $k$-Means on $X$, and runs in time $\tilde{O}\big{(}(jk)^{O(\min\{j,k\})}\cdot nd+2^{\mathrm{poly}(jk/\epsilon)}\cdot d^{O(1)}\big{)}$.
comments.explanation: The runtime expression omits the factor $\operatorname{poly}(\epsilon^{-1}d\log n)$ from the coreset size (Theorem 1.1). The PTAS has quadratic dependence on its input size, so the term $2^{\mathrm{poly}(jk/\epsilon)}$ should be multiplied by the square of the coreset size, not just by $d^{O(1)}$. The stated runtime $2^{\mathrm{poly}(jk/\epsilon)}\cdot d^{O(1)}$ would be correct only if the coreset size were independent of $\epsilon$, $d$, and $n$, which it is not. Rewrite the runtime as $\tilde{O}\big((jk)^{O(\min\{j,k\})}\cdot nd + 2^{\mathrm{poly}(jk/\epsilon)}\cdot (jk)^{O(\min\{j,k\})}\cdot (\epsilon^{-1}d\log n)^{O(1)}\big)$ to reflect the PTAS's quadratic dependence on the coreset size.

comments.title: PTAS speedup claim conflates near-linear with polynomial in log n
comments.quote: its time bound is *quadratic*, namely $O(2^{\mathrm{poly}(jk/\epsilon)}\cdot n^{2}d)$, and since our coreset can be constructed in near-linear time, we can speedup this PTAS to *near-linear* time by first constructing our coreset and then running this PTAS on the coreset.
comments.explanation: The claim that the combined algorithm runs in near-linear time is not justified. The PTAS has quadratic dependence on its input size. When run on the coreset of size $m$, its runtime becomes $O(2^{\mathrm{poly}(jk/\epsilon)}\cdot m^{2}d)$. Since $m$ itself is $(jk)^{O(\min(j,k))}\cdot(\epsilon^{-1}d\log n)^2$, the term $m^{2}$ introduces a factor $(jk)^{O(\min(j,k))}\cdot(\epsilon^{-1}d\log n)^4$, which is polynomial in $n$ and $d$ but far from near-linear. The overall runtime is dominated by $2^{\mathrm{poly}(jk/\epsilon)}\cdot (jk)^{O(\min(j,k))}\cdot \operatorname{poly}(\epsilon^{-1}d\log n)$, which is not near-linear in $n$ because the $\log n$ factor is raised to a power. The authors should clarify that the speedup is from quadratic to polynomial in $n$ (with a large constant), not to near-linear, unless they can show that the $2^{\mathrm{poly}(jk/\epsilon)}$ term dominates and the polynomial factors are negligible.

comments.title: Missing justification for importance sampling reduction in missing-values setting
comments.quote: The computation of $\{\sigma_{x}\}$ is standard in metric spaces, see e.g. *[x13, x10]*, but this is not applicable for us because distances with missing values do not satisfy the triangle inequality. Hence, we employ an alternative approach proposed by Varadarajan and Xiao *[x17, Lemma 3.1]*, which reduces the computation of $\{\sigma_{x}\}$ to finding coresets for $k$-Center.
comments.explanation: The paper invokes Varadarajan and Xiao's reduction without verifying that its conditions hold in the missing-values setting. The reduction in [x17, Lemma 3.1] assumes a metric space (triangle inequality) for the distance function. However, the paper explicitly states that distances with missing values do not satisfy the triangle inequality. The reduction is applied to restricted data sets $X_{|I}$ where all points share the same set of available coordinates $I$, which does form a metric space. But the paper does not state this justification explicitly. Without this clarification, a reader may doubt the validity of the reduction. Add a sentence explaining that the reduction is applied to restricted data sets that satisfy the triangle inequality.

comments.title: Unsupported claim about Gonzalez coreset size
comments.quote: We show that the resulting coreset, consisting of $k+1$ points, is an $\tilde{O}(1)$-coreset for $k$-Center.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that running an approximate Gonzalez algorithm for $k$ iterations yields a coreset of size $k+1$ that is an $\tilde{O}(1)$-coreset for $k$-Center. No proof or reference is provided for this claim. Standard results (e.g., Har-Peled and Mazumdar) show that Gonzalez's algorithm gives a 2-approximation for $k$-center, but using it as a coreset construction requires a different analysis. The claim that $k+1$ points suffice for an $\tilde{O}(1)$-coreset is non-trivial and should be justified. Add a brief justification or a reference to a known result (e.g., Lemma 4.2 in Har-Peled and Mazumdar, 2004).

comments.title: Empirical error definition uses cost difference incorrectly
comments.explanation: The definition of empirical error in equation (1) is problematic because it uses the absolute difference in costs divided by the original cost. For a coreset $D$ with weights $w$, the cost $\operatorname{cost}(D,C)$ should be the weighted cost $\sum_{x\in D} w(x) \cdot d(x,C)^z$, not the unweighted sum. The paper's Definition 2.2 of an $\epsilon$-coreset requires that for all center sets $C$, $(1-\epsilon)\operatorname{cost}(X,C) \leq \operatorname{cost}(D,C) \leq (1+\epsilon)\operatorname{cost}(X,C)$. The error measure in (1) uses the absolute difference, which is not equivalent to the multiplicative guarantee: a coreset satisfying the multiplicative guarantee would have error at most $\epsilon$, but the converse does not hold. More critically, the denominator $\operatorname{cost}(X,C)$ could be zero if $C$ is placed exactly at a data point, causing division by zero. The authors should either define the error as the maximum multiplicative deviation $\max\{ \frac{\operatorname{cost}(D,C)}{\operatorname{cost}(X,C)}, \frac{\operatorname{cost}(X,C)}{\operatorname{cost}(D,C)} \} - 1$ or clarify that they only consider center sets with positive cost. Additionally, the paper does not specify whether $\operatorname{cost}(D,C)$ is computed using the coreset weights or as an unweighted sum over the subset $D$ — this ambiguity makes the empirical evaluation uninterpretable.

comments.title: Center set generation method is not described
comments.quote: e a randomly selected collection of centers  $\mathcal{C}$  that consists of 100 randomly generated  $k$ -subset  $C \subset \mathbb{R}^d$ .
comments.explanation: The method for generating the random center sets is underspecified. The paper says 'randomly generated $k$-subset $C \subset \mathbb{R}^d$' but does not state the distribution from which these centers are drawn. If centers are drawn uniformly from the bounding box of the data, the resulting costs $\operatorname{cost}(X,C)$ could be extremely large, making the relative error in (1) artificially small and uninformative. Conversely, if centers are drawn near the data, the error might be larger. Without specifying the distribution, the empirical error values reported in Figure 2 are not reproducible. Specify the exact procedure (e.g., sample $k$ points uniformly from the data set, or sample each coordinate uniformly from the min-max range of that coordinate) and justify why this choice is appropriate for evaluating coreset quality.

comments.title: Inconsistent comparison of relative errors across figures
comments.quote: We note that relative errors for both our coreset and uniform sampling are significantly lower than that we observe from the empirical error in Figure 2a. In fact, they are not necessarily comparable since the empirical error in Figure 2a is always evaluated on a same center, while what we compare in Figure 4a is the center sets found by the modified Lloyd's running on different data sets.
comments.explanation: The text acknowledges that the errors in Figure 2a and Figure 4a are not comparable, but then uses Figure 2a to explain why uniform sampling fails in Figure 4a. Specifically, the sentence 'since as shown in Figure 2a, uniform sampling has a large empirical error (around 50%), so a good solution for the uniform sample may not be a good solution for the original data set' invokes Figure 2a to explain a phenomenon in Figure 4a, despite stating they are not comparable. This is logically inconsistent. The explanation for uniform sampling's poor performance in Figure 4a should be based on evidence from Figure 4a itself or a separate analysis, not on a figure that measures a different quantity. Rewrite the explanation to avoid referencing Figure 2a, or clarify how the two figures are related despite the stated incomparability.

comments.title: Missing justification for 5000 iterations of Lloyd's heuristic
comments.quote: The experiments are run on the Russian housing data set where the number of iterations of the modified Lloyd's is set to  $T = 5000$  and the number of clusters is set to a small value  $k = 3$  so as the heuristic is likely to find a local minimum faster.
comments.explanation: The claim that setting T=5000 iterations makes the heuristic 'likely to find a local minimum faster' is vague and unsupported. Lloyd's algorithm converges monotonically, but the number of iterations needed to reach a local minimum depends on the data and initialization, not on a fixed iteration cap. Moreover, 5000 iterations for k=3 on a dataset of moderate size is unusually high; typical implementations converge in tens to hundreds of iterations. Provide evidence that 5000 iterations are needed for convergence on this dataset, or explain why a smaller T would not suffice. Without justification, the choice of T=5000 appears arbitrary and may inflate the reported running time for the baseline, making the speedup from coresets look more dramatic than it actually is.

comments.title: Coreset construction time not fully accounted in speedup claim
comments.quote: A coreset of size only 1000 can achieve  $< 1\%$  error, and the running time of constructing the coreset and applying Lloyd's on top of it are 3s and 0.8s, respectively, which offers more than 5 times of speedup.
comments.explanation: The speedup calculation appears to compare the total time on coreset (3.8s) against the original Lloyd's time (22.9s), yielding roughly a 6x speedup. However, the text does not state whether the 22.9s for the original Lloyd's includes the same number of iterations (T=5000) as used for the coreset-based runs. If the original Lloyd's was run for fewer iterations (e.g., until convergence) while the coreset-based runs used 5000 iterations, the comparison is unfair. Explicitly state the number of iterations used for the original Lloyd's and ensure it matches the coreset-based runs. Additionally, the speedup factor should be computed as (original time) / (coreset construction + coreset Lloyd's time), which is 22.9 / 3.8 ≈ 6.0, not 'more than 5 times' — correct this for precision.

comments.title: KDD Cup dataset has j=30 but d=31, making j nearly equal to d
comments.explanation: The KDD Cup dataset has d=31 dimensions and j=30 missing coordinates per point, meaning each point can have at most 1 observed coordinate. In such a setting, the distance between two points can only be computed over at most 1 coordinate, making the clustering problem nearly degenerate. The coreset size bound (jk)^{O(min(j,k))} = (150)^{O(5)} is enormous, and the practical utility of the coreset in this extreme regime is questionable. Discuss whether the coreset construction remains meaningful when j is close to d, and whether the experimental results on this dataset are reliable (e.g., whether the Lloyd's heuristic can converge with so few observed coordinates).

comments.title: Synthetic dataset generation may not reflect realistic missingness
comments.quote: Data points are randomly generated so that  $97\%$  of them are in a square and  $3\%$  of them are far away from the square. After that, we delete  $25\%$  of attributes at random.
comments.explanation: The synthetic dataset is generated by placing 97% of points in a square and 3% far away, then deleting 25% of attributes at random. This creates a dataset with a clear outlier structure, but the missingness mechanism (MCAR) may not reflect realistic patterns where missingness depends on the data values or cluster membership. The paper's theoretical analysis assumes a worst-case missing pattern, but the synthetic evaluation uses a benign random missing pattern. Either justify why this synthetic setup is a valid test of the coreset's worst-case guarantees, or include a synthetic experiment with adversarial missing patterns to demonstrate robustness.

comments.title: Coreset size dependence on d is quadratic, not just low-degree
comments.quote: Our coreset size is only a low-degree polynomial of $d,\epsilon$ and $\log n$, and can thus deal with moderately-high dimension or large data set.
comments.explanation: The coreset size from Theorem 1.1 is $(jk)^{O(\min\{j,k\})}\cdot(\epsilon^{-1}d\log n)^2$, which is quadratic in $d$. While quadratic is technically a low-degree polynomial, the phrase 'only a low-degree polynomial' could mislead readers into expecting a milder dependence (e.g., linear or logarithmic). Rewrite the sentence to explicitly state the quadratic dependence on $d$ to avoid ambiguity. For example: 'Our coreset size is quadratic in $d$ and $\epsilon^{-1}$, and polylogarithmic in $n$, and can thus deal with moderately-high dimension or large data sets.'

comments.title: Imprecise denominator in distance lower bound
comments.quote: For $i\in[k]$, let $t_{i}\in\arg\max_{t\in I_{x}}|x_{t}-c^{i}_{t}|$, i.e., $t_{i}$ is the index of coordinate that contributes the most in distance $\mathrm{dist}(x,c^{i})$, so $|x_{t_{i}}-c^{i}_{t_{i}}|\geq\frac{r}{\sqrt{d}}$.
comments.explanation: The inequality $|x_{t_i} - c^i_{t_i}| \geq r/\sqrt{d}$ is valid because $\mathrm{dist}(x,c^i) \geq r$ and $|I_x| \leq d$, so $|x_{t_i} - c^i_{t_i}| \geq \mathrm{dist}(x,c^i)/\sqrt{|I_x|} \geq r/\sqrt{d}$. The reasoning should explicitly note that $\sqrt{d}$ is an upper bound on $\sqrt{|I_x|}$, not an equality. Rewrite as: 'so $|x_{t_i} - c^i_{t_i}| \geq \frac{\mathrm{dist}(x,c^i)}{\sqrt{|I_x|}} \geq \frac{r}{\sqrt{d}}$ because $|I_x| \leq d$.'

comments.title: Contradiction between Lemma 3.4 bound and experimental optimal family size
comments.quote: Although Lemma 3.4 gives a theoretical upper bound on  $|\mathcal{I}|$  but our experiments suggest that a much smaller size  $|\mathcal{I}| = 20$  is optimal in this case.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that Lemma 3.4 provides an upper bound on |I|, but the experiments show that |I|=20 is optimal. However, the theoretical bound from Lemma 3.4 is (jk)^{O(min(j,k))}, which for j=3, k=3 is (9)^{O(3)} = 9^c for some constant c, likely much larger than 20. This is not a contradiction—the theoretical bound is an upper bound, not a lower bound, so a smaller optimal value is expected. The phrasing 'although...but our experiments suggest' implies a tension that does not exist. Rephrase to clarify that the theoretical bound is loose for this dataset and that the experiments identify a practical choice of |I| that is far below the worst-case bound.
